coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive and selective “turn off” fluorescent sensor based on water soluble copper nanoclusters for morin and temperature sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Zhu, Ruitao
Zhang, Shen
Wu, Liangliang
Li, Junhong
Huang, Suiyuan
Ma, Xinru
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence detection method was developed for the determination of morin and temperature based on water soluble copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) for the first time, which was fabricated by using tannic acid (TA) as the protecting agent and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. The TA protected Cu NCs (TA-Cu NCs) were characterized through a series of methods. The characterization results showed that the as-prepared TA-Cu NCs possessed good water solubility and high photostability. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of TA-Cu NCs was obviously quenched in the presence of morin. Then, a rapid and facile morin probe was established based on TA-Cu NCs, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was ascribed to the inner filter effect. The as-prepared probe showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards morin with a wider linear range from 0.5 to 110 μM (R2 = 0.9949), and a lower detection limit could reach 0.062 μM. In addition, the TA-Cu NCs were employed for temperature sensing. The practicability of the TA-Cu NCs for morin detection in bovine serum samples was also verified with the recoveries range from 92.5% to 107.7%, illustrating this detection method with great potential application in the field of analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Tannic acid
Fluorescence nanoprobe
Morin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of morin in bovine serum samples
Synthesis and characterization of as-prepared TA-Cu NCs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the TA-Cu NCs for morin detection
Optimal conditions for fluorescent detection of morin
Selectivity study of TA-Cu NCs for morin assay
Practical application in bovine serum samples

Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of TA-Cu NCs (inset: picture of TA-Cu NCs under daylight lamp and UV lamp (365 nm)); (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of TA-Cu NCs, TA, AA and Cu(NO3)2; (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TA-Cu NCs, TA, AA and Cu(NO3)2; (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of TA-Cu NCs under different excitation wavelength (varying from 320 to 400 nm).
(A)TEM image of TA-Cu NCs; (B) FT-IR spectra of TA-Cu NCs and TA; (C) The XPS spectrum of TA-Cu NCs; (D) The XPS spectrum of Cu 2p.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of TA-Cu NCs, morin and TA-Cu NCs + morin mixture; (B) The fluorescence lifetime of TA-Cu NCs and TA-Cu NCs + morin system; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of morin, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of TA-Cu NCs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectrum of TA-Cu NCs in the presence of various morin concentrations (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130, 140 and 150 μM); (B) The linear relationship between F0/F value and the concentrations of morin (0.5–110 μM).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of TA-Cu NCs with morin and other reference substances (concentration = 150 μM); (B) F0/F of TA-Cu NCs solution with morin and other reference substances (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, Glu, Pro, Cys, Gly and Ala).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectrum of TA-Cu NCs at different temperature (from 25 to 80 °C); (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity of TA-Cu NCs at different temperature; (C) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature (30–70 °C); (D) The cycles of TA-Cu NCs for temperature sensing between 25 °C and 80 °C.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of TA-Cu NCs as a fluorescent sensing method for morin.
Comparison of different analytical methods for morin detection.
The morin recoveries in bovine serum samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND: not detectable; NC: not calculated.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive and selective “turn off” fluorescent sensor based on water soluble copper nanoclusters for morin and temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence detection method was developed for the determination of morin and temperature based on water soluble copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) for the first time, which was fabricated by using tannic acid (TA) as the protecting agent and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. The TA protected Cu NCs (TA-Cu NCs) were characterized through a series of methods. The characterization results showed that the as-prepared TA-Cu NCs possessed good water solubility and high photostability. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of TA-Cu NCs was obviously quenched in the presence of morin. Then, a rapid and facile morin probe was established based on TA-Cu NCs, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was ascribed to the inner filter effect. The as-prepared probe showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards morin with a wider linear range from 0.5 to 110 μM (R2 = 0.9949), and a lower detection limit could reach 0.062 μM. In addition, the TA-Cu NCs were employed for temperature sensing. The practicability of the TA-Cu NCs for morin detection in bovine serum samples was also verified with the recoveries range from 92.5% to 107.7%, illustrating this detection method with great potential application in the field of analysis.

In this work, a novel fluorescence sensor for morin and temperature sensing was developed based on the Cu NCs by using tannic acid as the protecting agent and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Morin, 2-(2,4-Dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxy-4H-chromen-4-one dehydrate, is derived from bark of yellow mulberry, mulberry orange tree and many Chinese herbal medicines [1,2]. It possesses antibacterial effect, antiviral effect, anticancer effect and antispasmodic effect and can be used to treat headaches, stomach problems, chronic inflammations and coronary heart disease [3–5]. Recently, it has been slowly employed to treat cancer, which can destroy the morphology and proliferation of cell lines such as liver cancer and esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, and further interfere with the division of cancer cells and inhibit their DNA synthesis [6,7]. However, excessive use of morin can produce some side reactions. Thence, it is very necessary to accurately determine the concentration of morin. Currently, many detection methods have been employed to determine morin including high performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet (HPLC-UV) [8], high-performance liquid chromatography-diode array detection (HPLC-DAD) [9], electrochemical methods [10–12], ultraviolet spectrophotometry [13], capillary electrophoresis [14] and fluorescence methods [15,16]. Among them, traditional analytical methods possess high sensitivity and low detection limit, they have disadvantages of the expensive instruments, professional operators and time consuming [17,18]. Electrochemical methods demand complex electrode probes and its selectivity is poor. Unlike above techniques, fluorescence methods offer a promising approach for simple, low-costly and rapid tracking morin in real samples. In recent years, due to its simplicity, high sensitivity and easy operation, fluorescence methods have been successfully employed to detect morin [15,16]. The main fluorescent probes are carbon dots (CDs). However, they need higher preparation temperature. Therefore, the development of simple fluorescent probes is necessary.

It has been reported that fluorescent materials with strong fluorescence and large Stokes shift are employed for the detection of small biomolecules and cell imaging [19,20]. Among them, metal nanoclusters are nano-sized fluorescent materials and possess excellent optical and physio-chemical properties including strong fluorescence, good biocompatibility, large Stokes shift, low toxicity, high photostability, high salt resistance and good catalytic activity [21–27], which have been applied in different fields, such as chemical detection, biometrics, cell imaging, targeted drug carrier and catalysis [28–30]. Metal nanoclusters mainly include gold nanoclusters, silver nanoclusters and copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs). Compared to gold and silver, copper reserves are relatively high in nature and its price is cheaper [31,32]. Therefore, Cu NCs have attracted more attention for drug sensing, cell imaging and biometrics. So far, numerous methods have been widely used to prepare Cu NCs including electrochemical synthesis methods [33], microwave-assisted synthesis methods [34], template-assisted synthesis methods [35] and photoreduction synthesis methods [36]. At the same time, many substances containing active groups, such as protein (bovine serum albumin [37], trypsin [38], papain [39] and lysozyme [40]), polymer [41], amino acid (histidine [42], tyrosine [43], proline [44], cysteine [45]) and tannic acid (TA) [46], have been used to modify Cu NCs. Among them, TA is a large molecular weight organic substance and possesses a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which can improve the stability and water solubility of Cu NCs. Until now, TA stabilized Cu NCs (TA-Cu NCs) have been developed and applied for the determination of nitrite [47], phosphate ion [48], ferric ion [49], ascorbic acid (AA) [50] and dopamine [51]. Nevertheless, the reports of TA-Cu NCs as fluorescent probes for drugs sensing are very few, the applications of TA-Cu NCs in this area is still in its infancy. Moreover, there are few reports about the detection of morin and temperature sensing based on Cu NCs in the literatures.

Inner filter effect (IFE) is usually considered as an error in fluorescent measurement. In recent years, IFE has gained much attention in the field of sensors, due to its simplicity, flexibility and substantially enhanced sensitivity and selectivity [52]. IFE can be caused by the absorption spectrum of the analyte overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of fluorophores. Designing of IFE based sensors are flexible and it does not require the surface modification of the metal nanoclusters or establishing of any covalent linking between the receptor and a fluorophore [52].

In this paper, we developed a novel method for morin detection based on blue emissive Cu NCs, which were prepared by using TA as the protecting agent and AA as the reducing agent. More importantly, the TA-Cu NCs have been employed as fluorescent nanosensors to establish a detection system for efficient morin determination based on the fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the quenching mechanism was ascribed to the IFE between TA-Cu NCs and morin. The schematic illustration of the TA-Cu NCs synthesis and their applications as fluorescent nanoprobes for the determination of morin were displayed in Scheme 1 . In addition, the TA-Cu NCs were employed for temperature sensing. The practicability of the established morin probe for bovine serum samples analysis was also verified through the added recoveries.

The fluorescence spectra were taken on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo Japan), the Ex/Em slits were set at 5.0/5.0 nm and the scanning speed was 1200 nm min−1. The spectra of fluorescence lifetime were obtained on FLS-1000 combined fluorescence lifetime and steadystate spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded through Shimadzu 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on FTIR-8400S (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by using FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (United States) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were acquired through ESCALAB 250XI (United States). All pH measurements were recorded via a precision acidity FE20 pH-meter (Mettler Toledo Instrument Inc., Shanghai, China).

All reagents were of at least analytical grade without further purification. Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 99%), AA (99.5%) and TA (99%) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. Potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O, 99%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3·10H2O, 99%), glutamic acid (Glu, 99%), proline (Pro, 99%), cysteine (Cys, 99%), glycine (Gly, ≥98.5%), alanine (Ala, 99%) and morin (＞90%) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were prepared by mixing the appropriate volume of K2HPO4 and KH2PO4. Ultra-pure water (18.25 M cm−1) was purified by using a Millipore Milli-Q.

The TA-protected Cu NCs were prepared through a simple, green one-pot synthetic strategy [48]. Typically, Cu(NO3)2 solution (200 μL, 100 mM) and TA solution (100 μL, 1.0 mM) were separately added into round-bottomed flask containing 20 mL water under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 5 min. Thus, the AA solution (500 μL, 0.4 M) was quickly dropped into the above solution, and the mixture solution was reacted at 50 °C for 6 h. The solutions gradually changed from light blue to colorless, demonstrating the successful preparation of Cu NCs. Afterwards, the Cu NCs solution were purified by using a dialysis membrane (MW: 3000D) at room temperature for 24 h. The purified Cu NCs were stored under 4 °C for further use.

A typical fluorescence analysis of TA-Cu NCs for the detection of morin was shown as follows. A series of different concentrations of morin were added into 1.0 mL of TA-Cu NCs and 1.0 mL of PBS (6.5) mixture through a micro pipette (final morin concentrations were 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130, 140 and 150 μM, respectively). Then, the mixture were incubated at room temperature for 1.0 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra and intensities were recorded at emission wavelength of 434 nm (the Ex/Em slits were set at 5.0/5.0 nm).

In addition, to discuss the fluorescence selectivity of TA-Cu NCs towards morin, different possible references were added into a series of TA-Cu NCs solution and the fluorescence intensities were recorded under same conditions. The main processes were displayed as follows: different possible references (10 μL, 0.01 M), including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, Glu, Pro, Cys, Gly and Ala, were separately added into a series of TA-Cu NCs and PBS (6.5) aqueous solution (2 mL), the detection solutions were completely incubated at room temperature for 1.0 min. Furthermore, the morin solution (10 μL, 0.01 M) was added into the detection solutions containing different possible references for the interference experiments, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of morin in bovine serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practical application of TA-Cu NCs as a fluorescent probe in actual environment, a series of related experiments were studied. The bovine serum samples were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). First of all, the purchased bovine serum samples were diluted 100 times with PBS solution. Moreover, different standard concentrations of morin (10, 20 and 30 μM) were added into the diluted bovine serum samples, and the fluorescence spectra and recovery tests were obtained from follow-up experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of as-prepared TA-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of TA-stabilized Cu NCs and the fluorescence detection process of morin were illustrated in Scheme 1. Meanwhile, the optical performances, structure and composition of TA-Cu NCs were studied by using fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, TEM, FT-IR and XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of TA-Cu NCs were 366 and 434 nm, and the TA-Cu NCs solution displayed colorless and blue under daylight lamp and UV lamp (365 nm), respectively (inset Fig. 1A). Moreover, the fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of TA-Cu NCs, TA, AA and Cu(NO3)2 were taken on fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, respectively (Fig.1B and C). As shown in Fig. 1B, a distinct peak originated from TA-Cu NCs at 434 nm was observed, while the fluorescence spectra of TA, AA and Cu(NO3)2 displayed a straight line. As displayed in Fig. 1C, the UV–vis absorption spectra demonstrated that the TA-Cu NCs solution had an obvious absorption peak at 375 nm. The effect of the fluorescence excitation wavelength on the fluorescence emission wavelength was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 1D, with the fluorescence excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 400 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelength was not constant, indicating the excitation-dependent properties of TA-Cu NCs.

The morphology properties of TA-Cu NCs were discussed through TEM images and showed in Fig. 2 A. TA-Cu NCs were well dispersed with a size of 2.5 nm and no large copper nanoparticles or aggregation emerged because of the protective effect of TA. FT-IR spectra showed that TA-Cu NCs and TA possessed similar peak positions and main functional groups, indicating the successful introduction of TA onto TA-Cu NCs (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, XPS was also applied for the analysis of the component of TA-Cu NCs and the state of copper. As shown in Fig. 2C, the TA-Cu NCs possessed three elements, such as C, O and Cu. The state of copper was displayed in Fig. 2D, the XPS spectrum of Cu 2p possessed two peaks at 932.2 and 952.1 eV, which represented Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively. Moreover, the peak of Cu2+ at 942.0 eV was not observed, suggesting that Cu2+ was absolutely reduced. The above characterization results displayed that the TA-Cu NCs were successfully prepared by this simple and rapid strategy (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the TA-Cu NCs for morin detection
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching mainly include static quenching, dynamic quenching, IFE, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fluorescent quenching mechanisms of TA-Cu NCs in the presence of morin were explored through UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence spectroscopy. Firstly, the UV–vis absorption spectra of TA-Cu NCs, morin and TA-Cu NCs + morin mixture were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, compared with TA-Cu NCs and morin, a new absorption peak was not observed in TA-Cu NCs + morin mixture, revealing that no new substance was formed. Meanwhile, the FT-IR spectra of TA-Cu NCs, morin and TA-Cu NCs + morin were displayed in Fig. S1. Compared to TA-Cu NCs, morin, no obvious change was observed in the spectrum of TA-Cu NCs + morin, which proved that no new substance was formed. The formation of non-fluorescent ground state complexes could be used to prove static quenching and alter the UV–vis absorption spectra of TA-Cu NCs. Thence, the quenching mechanism was not attributed to static quenching. Additionally, we investigated the fluorescence lifetime of TA-Cu NCs and TA-Cu NCs + morin mixture. The spectra of fluorescence lifetime of TA-Cu NCs and TA-Cu NCs + morin mixture were displayed in Fig. 3B. The average fluorescence lifetime was calculated based on the equation: τaverage = (B1t1 2+B2t2 2)/(B1t1+B2t2) [53,54]. Namely, the average fluorescence lifetime of TA-Cu NCs was 1.74 ns, the time constants were 0.98 ns (60.22%) and 2.88 ns (39.78%). The average fluorescence lifetime of TA-Cu NCs + morin mixture was 1.73 ns, the time constants were 1.04 ns (65.35%) and 3.10 ns (34.65%). Fluorescence lifetime decay was less than 1 ns, which excluded dynamic quenching and FRET. As seen in Fig. 3C, an overlapping phenomenon was observed between the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of TA-Cu NCs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of morin. The above characterization results displayed that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of TA-Cu NCs in the presence of morin should be attributed to the IFE [55].

As far as we know, the stability of fluorescent probe was an important role for exploring their further applications. To check the stability of the probe, the fluorescence intensities of TA-Cu NCs were also discussed under different conditions (storage time, UV light irradiation time, ionic strength and solvents). It could be clearly seen from Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity was highly stable after the storage time of 30 d. Furthermore, the photostability was one of the main features for metal nanoclusters, which could increase their scope of application in labeling, sensing and bioimaging. From this angle, the photostability of Cu NCs were observed under UV lamp and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fig. S2B showed the fluorescence intensities of TA-Cu NCs under constant ultraviolet light (λ = 365 nm) for 30 min. As displayed in Fig. S2B, the changes in fluorescence intensity were not observed, which clearly revealed that the TA-Cu NCs possessed excellent photostability. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of TA-Cu NCs remained extremely stable under high ionic strength (the concentration of NaCl even reached 0.50 M) (Fig. S2C). The effect of different solvents on the stability of TA-Cu NCs was also investigated including methanol, ethanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), isopropanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and acetone. As shown in Fig. S2D, no significant changes of fluorescence intensity were observed under different solvents (1 mL TA-Cu NCs+1 mL solvent). The above stability experiment results illustrated that TA-Cu NCs possessed excellent stability under long-term storage, long-term UV irradiation, high ionic strength and different solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for fluorescent detection of morin
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain more sensitive determination of morin, the detection conditions of TA-Cu NCs towards morin were optimized such as pH value, incubation time and incubation temperature. Firstly, the effect of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of detection system was studied with PBS solution (varying from 5.7 to 8.0). As shown in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity difference (F0–F) of the TA-Cu NCs + morin system gradually decreased with pH varying from 5.7 to 8.0, then we selected 6.0 as the detection pH value for the further determination experiments of morin. Moreover, after adding morin, the fluorescence intensity difference (F0–F) of the TA-Cu NCs-morin system were obtained under different incubation time (from 0 to 150 s) and incubation temperature (from 25 to 50 °C). As displayed in Fig. S3B and Fig. S3C, the fluorescence intensity difference (F0–F) of the detection system reached maximum under 1.0 min and 30 °C, which indicated that a rapid detection platform for morin could be developed. (F0 and F represents the fluorescence intensity of TA-Cu NCs in the absence and presence of morin).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of TA-Cu NCs for morin assay
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, we developed a fluorescence probe by using TA-Cu NCs for the determination of morin. The interference experiments of other substances to our as-prepared fluorescence sensor should be studied by recording the fluorescence intensity change in the presence of various reference substances, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, Glu, Pro, Cys, Gly and Ala. The fluorescence emission spectra and corresponding relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) in the presence of morin and various reference substances were displayed in Fig. 5 A and Fig. 5B, respectively. In addition, the color changes of TA-Cu NCs solution in the presence of morin and various reference substances under UV lamp were demonstrated in inset Fig. 5B. As seen in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence emission spectra of the TA-Cu NCs at 434 nm decreased significantly only in the presence of morin. On the other hand, other reference substances did not affect the fluorescence intensity. Moreover, it could be seen from Fig. 5B, the color of the TA-Cu NCs solution under UV lamp changed from dark blue to light blue only in the case of morin, illustrating that the as-prepared TA-Cu NCs for morin possessed excellent selectivity. Finally, the morin was added into the TA-Cu NCs solution in the presence of reference substances. The interference experiment results were displayed in Fig. S4. The fluorescence intensity remained constant in the presence of reference substances, while the fluorescence was quenched after addition of morin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in bovine serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the application of this developed method in real samples, we further studied the feasibility of this method for morin determination in bovine serum samples. The experimental results were displayed in Table 2 . As seen in Table 2, the measured values were very close to the spiked values, and the recoveries of morin were in the range of 92.5–107.7%. These experimental results showed that the proposed fluorescence nanosensor based on the TA-Cu NCs provided great potential for the detection of morin in bovine serum samples.

To investigate the possibility of the as-prepared TA-Cu NCs for temperature sensing, we studied the temperature response of TA-Cu NCs. The fluorescence intensities of TA-Cu NCs were recorded at different temperature (from 25 to 80 °C), and the results were displayed in Fig. 6 . As seen in Fig. 6A and B, with increasing the temperature, the intensity gradually decreased, indicating that the fluorescence of TA-Cu NCs could be quenched by high temperature. The main reason was attributed to the increase of molecular collision frequency and non-radiative transition rate, but the radiation transition rate remained constant at high temperature, which resulting in the decrease of emission intensity from excited state [56,57]. It could be seen from Fig. 6C, there was an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the temperature in the range of 30–70 °C. The linear fitting equation of the fluorescence detection system was F = −2.17[Temperature] + 291.64 (R2 = 0.9975). Interestingly, when the temperature dropped to 25 °C, the fluorescence intensity of TA-Cu NCs recovered. As shown in Fig. 6D, the temperature probe based on TA-Cu NCs could be duplicated for nine uninterrupted cycles and the FL intensity only weakened slightly.

In summary, a facile and rapid fluorescent “turn off” strategy for the detection of morin was developed by using TA-Cu NCs as fluorescent nanoprobe. The “turn off” detection platform possessed the advantages of excellent sensitivity and high selectivity. Under optimal detection conditions, an excellent linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.5–110 μM, and the lower LOD was 0.062 μM. In addition, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of TA-Cu NCs to morin was attributed to the IFE. The proposed method was successfully used for the detection of temperature, morin in bovine serum samples with excellent accuracy and good repeatability.

Zhifeng Cai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Ruitao Zhu, Liangliang Wu: measure fluorescence. Junhong Li: measure UV-visible spectra. Suiyuan Huang: performing the experiments. Xinru Ma: writing. Shen Zhang: reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (No. 201801D121257), the College Students' Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2004), the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2020L0499), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project for College Students in Shanxi Province (No. 2020486) and the Fund for Shanxi “1331 Project” Collaborative Innovation Center (No. I018038) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.118108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quercetin inhibits the growth of a multidrug-resistant estrogen-receptor-negative mcf-7 human breast-cancer cell-line expressing type-Ii estrogen-binding sites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Scambia, F.O. Ranelletti, P.B. Panici, M. Piantelli, G. Bonanno, R. Devincenzo, G. Ferrandina, L. Pierelli, A. Capelli, S.
